_README.purpose: Translations for worksheet 02 (Standby vs. OFF — Hidden Energy Consumption).
_README.language: English — reference file. Original content authored by the Portuguese partner.
_README.instructions: All other languages copy this file and translate the values. Keys (left of the colon) must never change. See translator-guide.html for full instructions.

meta._comment: The header block of the worksheet.
meta.title: Standby vs. OFF — Hidden Energy Consumption
meta.topic: 01: Efficient Use of Energy — Entertainment & Electronics

objectives._comment: Learning outcomes — three bullet points in the structural table.
objectives.o2: Compare power usage in different device states.
objectives.o3: Calculate annual energy waste and its cost.

structural._comment: Other rows of the structural table.
structural.resources: TV / console / monitor specifications (or internet), calculator, power meter (optional).

introduction._comment: Introductory text after the structural table.
introduction.p1: Many electronic devices continue to consume energy even when they are "turned off" — as long as they remain plugged in. This is called standby power, or more colourfully, vampire energy. A small LED, a clock display, or a wireless receiver waiting for a remote signal all draw a few watts continuously.
introduction.p2: Individually it seems trivial. Across a household — TVs, game consoles, monitors, chargers, microwaves — it adds up to a noticeable share of the electricity bill, and to wasted carbon emissions. In this worksheet you will quantify exactly how much is wasted and what it costs.

formula._comment: Annual energy formula box. The math itself is hard-coded in KaTeX; only the labels translate.
formula.intro: The formula below converts continuous power draw into yearly energy use. For standby, assume the device sits in standby for 20 hours per day.
formula.varH: hours per day in that state

activities._comment: Five student tasks. Each has a sub-heading and an intro line; some have prompt lines and table headers.

a1._comment: Task 1 — Identify three devices at home.
a1.intro: List three devices in your home that go into standby (a small LED stays on when you press "off").

a2._comment: Task 2 — Power comparison table. Column headers and three pre-filled device rows.
a2.intro: Look up each device's power in three states. Use the user manual, the back label, an online datasheet, or a power meter if you have one.

a3._comment: Task 3 — Annual consumption calculation. Two lines with blank fields.
a3.intro: Take the TV from your table. Use the formula to calculate its yearly standby energy.

a4._comment: Task 4 — Cost calculation. Two lines with blank fields.
a4.intro: Find your local electricity price per kWh and calculate the yearly cost of that TV standby.

a5._comment: Task 5 — Real-world scenario. Single estimation line.
a5.intro: Imagine a typical home with 10 devices sitting in standby. Estimate the total annual cost (you may assume an average standby of 5 W per device).

didYouKnow._comment: Green tip-box near the end of the worksheet.
didYouKnow.title: 💡 Did you know?
didYouKnow.body: Turning off devices at the wall socket — instead of leaving them on standby — can save up to 10 % of household electricity. A switched power strip lets you cut several devices at once with a single flick.

reflection._comment: Open-ended writing prompt at the bottom of the worksheet.
reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.prompt: Explain in your own words why standby energy is sometimes called "invisible consumption", and how it affects the energy use of your own home.

ui._comment: Generic interface labels reused across worksheets — same in many sheets.
